messages.content:
This is an updated resubmission of my complaint regarding case PSIC-D-2025-1112. My understanding of the decision letter is that the PSIC does not review allegations of wrongdoing committed by CBSA against specific individuals. Based on this interpretation, in addition to my own case, I have gathered information from five other Study Permit applicants (details appended at the end of this complaint). I am submitting this complaint on behalf of all six of us.
We share the following characteristics:
NaN.  All six of us are Chinese citizens.
    
NaN.  All our Study Permit applications were processed by the IRCC Beijing Office.
    
NaN.  CSIS has completed the portion of security screening under its mandate for each of our cases and has transmitted its assessment advice to CBSA/IRCC.
    
NaN.  Our security screenings are now stalled at CBSA, handled by the [China and Americas] group, within the Centre for Immigration National Security Screening, Security Screening and Refugee Directorate, Intelligence and Enforcement Branch (IEB), CBSA.
    
NaN.  All six of us fall under the VIT 34 category of security screening.
    
We hereby submit a complaint regarding wrongdoing by the [China and Americas] group of the Centre for Immigration National Security Screening, within the Security Screening and Refugee Directorate of the CBSA’s Intelligence and Enforcement Branch (IEB), in its handling of security screenings for Chinese Study Permit applicants.
Since CSIS transmitted its assessment advice to CBSA, our cases have seen no discernible progress for periods ranging from three months to over ten months. We have no knowledge of whether CBSA is actively processing our files, at what stage the processing currently stands, or when we can reasonably expect completion. Even direct inquiries to the Vice President of IEB have yielded nothing beyond generic statements that our cases are “ongoing” and that any further questions should be directed to IRCC.
According to the publicly available _CBSA Immigration Control (IC) Manual Chapter 1 – Security Screening Process Manual_ (https://www.getgcms.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2020/08/Release-Package-A-2018-11867.pdf), our cases fall under Chapter 10, _Screening Guidelines for Temporary Resident Applications_. The maximum processing times listed on page 24 of the manual are all within 20 days. Our waiting periods far exceed CBSA’s own stated standards. We therefore request that CBSA complete our security screenings without further delay and provide written confirmation once the results have been transmitted to IRCC.
I would also like to clarify an issue referenced in PSIC-D-2025-1112 concerning Access to Information and Privacy (ATIP). Our concern is not that CBSA has failed to respond to ATIP requests, but rather that CBSA ATIP provides almost no meaningful information regarding the status of our security screenings. This does not fall under the jurisdiction of the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada, as CBSA has in fact responded to our ATIP requests on time. However, the only information we consistently receive is a single GCMS Note entry: under _ADMISSIBILITIES – SECURITY – SUB ACTIVITIES: 1_, the status is marked **“Received by CBSA”**. This line provides no indication of whether CBSA is actively reviewing our cases or any detail about the progress. Moreover, CBSA’s security screening activities—focused primarily on travel history and general criminality checks—are substantially less sensitive than the national security assessments conducted by CSIS. These findings should not be withheld wholesale on national-security grounds.
Also please note the following:
NaN.  In this submission we are reporting wrongdoing affecting six individuals, in an effort to meet the threshold for systemic wrongdoing. If you still consider this to constitute wrongdoing against individuals, I can attempt to gather additional applicants. In that case, please advise how many affected persons would typically constitute a matter of systemic rather than individual wrongdoing.
    
NaN.  IRCC repeatedly responds with template messages stating that our files are in the security screening stage and that IRCC is awaiting “Partner Information” (i.e., CBSA and CSIS). CBSA and IRCC continue to redirect responsibility to each other. We understand that PSIC does not oversee IRCC, and we are only asking for oversight of CBSA’s responsibilities—specifically, CBSA’s screening for temporary resident applications. We are aware that immigration-related issues outside CBSA’s mandate must be raised through other channels. However, we respectfully request that CBSA not use IRCC’s role in processing Study Permit applications as justification to avoid their own obligations.
    
把这封信改成给The National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians的投诉信。现在我们人数扩充到了15人。首先开宗明义说我知道NSICOP不受理个人投诉，但我代表的是16名UBC被录取的学生，向NSICOP投诉CBSA拖延不作为。我们的的学签申请都被安调了，但CSIS已经完成了我们的Security Assessment并且把案子转给了CBSA。但半年过去了CBSA对我们的安调案子没有任何进展且拒不提供安调进度有关信息。
  
我们有向CBSA内部投诉渠道投诉，也向NSIRA PSIC投诉过CBSA，他们都不受理（如果你们需要书面不受理证据的话我也有）。Public Complaints and Review Commission也还没成立。所以NSICOP是目前唯一能监管CBSA的机构。我恳请NSICOP审查CBSA对中国籍学生安调案子的系统性主观不作为。

Here is the revised complaint letter addressed to **The National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP)**.

I have restructured the letter to meet the specific mandate of NSICOP. Since NSICOP focuses on the **efficacy, accountability, and compliance** of national security activities (rather than individual grievances), the letter emphasizes that your group represents a **systemic failure** and an **accountability gap** within the Canadian national security apparatus that no other agency is currently addressing.

***

**Subject:** Report of Systemic Inaction and Lack of Accountability within the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) regarding Security Screening of International Students

**To:**
The Secretariat of the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP)
Ottawa, Ontario
Canada

**Date:** [Insert Date]

**Dear Members of the Committee,**

I am writing to formally bring to your attention a matter of systemic inefficiency and lack of accountability within the **Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)**, specifically within the Intelligence and Enforcement Branch (IEB).

I am fully aware that the mandate of the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP) is to review the legislative, regulatory, policy, administrative, and financial framework for national security and intelligence, and that the Committee does not typically investigate individual complaints.

However, I am submitting this report on behalf of **16 individuals**—all admitted students to the **University of British Columbia (UBC)**—to demonstrate a pattern of systemic inaction that falls squarely within NSICOP’s mandate to review the **efficacy and appropriateness** of intelligence activities. We believe there is currently a regulatory vacuum where the CBSA is operating without oversight regarding the timeliness and accountability of its security screening mandates.

### Exhaustion of Other Review Mechanisms
We turn to NSICOP as a measure of last resort because all other avenues for oversight have been exhausted or are unavailable:
1.  **Internal CBSA Complaints:** Have yielded no substantive response.
2.  **NSIRA (National Security and Intelligence Review Agency):** Has declined to review our complaints.
3.  **PSIC (Public Sector Integrity Commissioner):** Has declined jurisdiction (Case Ref: PSIC-D-2025-1112), stating they do not review allegations of wrongdoing against specific individuals, even when presented as a group issue.
4.  **PCRC (Public Complaints and Review Commission):** This body, intended to provide oversight for CBSA, is not yet operational.

As such, **NSICOP is currently the only body with the jurisdiction** to review what we allege to be systemic, subjective inaction by the CBSA.

### The Systemic Issue
The 16 individuals represented in this complaint share the following characteristics:
*   We are all Chinese citizens admitted to the University of British Columbia.
*   Our applications are processed by the IRCC Beijing Office.
*   We fall under the **VIT 34** category of security screening.
*   **Crucially: CSIS has completed its security assessment mandates** and has transmitted its advice to the CBSA.

Our files are now stalled at the **Centre for Immigration National Security Screening**, specifically within the **[China and Americas] group** of the CBSA’s Security Screening and Refugee Directorate.

While CSIS has fulfilled its duties, the CBSA has failed to process these files for periods ranging from **six months to over ten months** *after* receiving the data from CSIS.

### Evidence of Maladministration and Lack of Accountability
We allege that the CBSA is engaged in systemic delays that violate its own operational guidelines and lack transparency:

**1. Violation of Service Standards:**
According to the *CBSA Immigration Control (IC) Manual Chapter 1 – Security Screening Process Manual*, our cases fall under *Chapter 10, Screening Guidelines for Temporary Resident Applications*. The stated processing standards are generally within 20 days. Our wait times now exceed **half a year** with zero movement. This suggests a breakdown in the administrative framework governing the IEB.

**2. The "Black Box" of Accountability:**
Through Access to Information and Privacy (ATIP) requests, we have confirmed that our status is marked as **“Received by CBSA”** under *ADMISSIBILITIES – SECURITY – SUB ACTIVITIES: 1*. This status has remained unchanged for months.
*   CSIS has done their job.
*   IRCC states they are waiting for "Partner Information" (CBSA).
*   CBSA refuses to provide updates, directing inquiries back to IRCC.
This circular shifting of responsibility creates a lack of accountability where CBSA’s Intelligence and Enforcement Branch effectively operates without a deadline or oversight.

**3. Withholding of Non-Sensitive Information:**
CBSA screenings primarily verify travel history and general criminality—activities far less sensitive than the national security assessments already completed by CSIS. Withholding the status of these administrative checks on "national security grounds" is an inappropriate application of security secrecy to cover administrative inaction.

### Request for Review
We respectfully request that NSICOP, under its mandate to review the **efficacy** of national security and intelligence activities, examine the following:

1.  **The operational efficiency of the CBSA’s [China and Americas] group** within the Centre for Immigration National Security Screening. specifically regarding the "holding" of files after CSIS assessments are complete.
2.  **The systemic delays** facing Chinese Study Permit applicants (specifically VIT 34 cases) and whether these delays constitute a policy of "subjective inaction" rather than legitimate security processing.
3.  **The lack of an oversight mechanism** for CBSA security screening delays, given that the PCRC is not active and NSIRA/PSIC have declined jurisdiction.

We are prepared to provide the specific file numbers, the correspondence from PSIC/NSIRA declining jurisdiction, and the ATIP records for all 16 applicants upon request.

We urge the Committee to investigate why the CBSA is failing to meet its mandate in a timely manner, thereby jeopardizing the education of high-potential students and the reputation of Canada’s immigration system.

Respectfully,

[Your Name]
[Your Phone Number]
[Your Email Address]
*On behalf of 16 admitted students to the University of British Columbia*

I am writing to formally bring to your attention a matter of systemic inefficiency and lack of accountability within the **Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)**, specifically within the Intelligence and Enforcement Branch (IEB).
这里第一段就写我要代表15名，引起他们的重视

没问题，这是一个非常关键的修改。在第一段直接抛出具体人数（16人），能够立刻向NSICOP表明这不是一个琐碎的个人个案，而是一个具备规模的群体性事件，从而更有可能符合他们审查“系统性问题”的职权范围。

以下是修改后的开头段落，以及为了配合这一改动而微调后的完整信件：

**Revised First Paragraph (修改后的第一段):**

> "I am writing to formally bring to your attention a matter of systemic inefficiency and lack of accountability within the **Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)**, specifically within the Intelligence and Enforcement Branch (IEB). **I submit this report on behalf of a collective of 16 admitted students to the University of British Columbia (myself and 15 others)** to demonstrate that this is not an isolated grievance, but a widespread failure in processing protocols that warrants the Committee's urgent review."

***

**Full Updated Letter (完整修改后的信件):**

**To:**
The Secretariat of the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP)
Ottawa, Ontario
Canada

**Date:** [Insert Date]

**Subject:** Report of Systemic Inaction and Lack of Accountability within the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) regarding Security Screening of International Students

**Dear Members of the Committee,**

I am writing to formally bring to your attention a matter of systemic inefficiency and lack of accountability within the **Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)**, specifically within the Intelligence and Enforcement Branch (IEB). **I submit this report on behalf of a collective of 16 admitted students to the University of British Columbia (myself and 15 others)** to demonstrate that this is not an isolated grievance, but a widespread failure in processing protocols that warrants the Committee's urgent review.

I am fully aware that the mandate of the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP) is to review the legislative, regulatory, policy, administrative, and financial framework for national security and intelligence, and that the Committee does not typically investigate individual complaints.

However, we are approaching NSICOP to report a **systemic administrative failure**. We allege that the CBSA is operating in a regulatory vacuum, engaging in subjective inaction regarding security screenings for Chinese students, with no effective oversight mechanism currently in place to hold them accountable.

### Exhaustion of Other Review Mechanisms
We turn to NSICOP as a measure of last resort because all other avenues for oversight have been exhausted or are unavailable:
1.  **Internal CBSA Complaints:** Have yielded no substantive response.
2.  **NSIRA (National Security and Intelligence Review Agency):** Has declined to review our complaints.
3.  **PSIC (Public Sector Integrity Commissioner):** Has declined jurisdiction (Case Ref: PSIC-D-2025-1112), stating they do not review allegations of wrongdoing against specific individuals, even when presented as a group issue.
4.  **PCRC (Public Complaints and Review Commission):** This body, intended to provide oversight for CBSA, is not yet operational.
*(We can provide written evidence of these refusals upon request.)*

As such, **NSICOP is currently the only body with the jurisdiction** to review what we allege to be systemic, subjective inaction by the CBSA.

### The Systemic Issue
The **16 individuals** represented in this complaint share the following characteristics:
*   We are all Chinese citizens admitted to the University of British Columbia.
*   Our applications are processed by the IRCC Beijing Office.
*   We fall under the **VIT 34** category of security screening.
*   **Crucially: CSIS has completed its security assessment mandates** and has transmitted its advice to the CBSA.

Our files are now stalled at the **Centre for Immigration National Security Screening**, specifically within the **[China and Americas] group** of the CBSA’s Security Screening and Refugee Directorate.

While CSIS has fulfilled its duties, the CBSA has failed to process these files for periods ranging from **six months to over ten months** *after* receiving the data from CSIS.

### Evidence of Maladministration and Lack of Accountability
We allege that the CBSA is engaged in systemic delays that violate its own operational guidelines and lack transparency:

**1. Violation of Service Standards:**
According to the *CBSA Immigration Control (IC) Manual Chapter 1 – Security Screening Process Manual*, our cases fall under *Chapter 10, Screening Guidelines for Temporary Resident Applications*. The stated processing standards are generally within 20 days. Our wait times now exceed **half a year** with zero movement. This suggests a breakdown in the administrative framework governing the IEB.

**2. The "Black Box" of Accountability:**
Through Access to Information and Privacy (ATIP) requests, we have confirmed that our status is marked as **“Received by CBSA”** under *ADMISSIBILITIES – SECURITY – SUB ACTIVITIES: 1*. This status has remained unchanged for months.
*   CSIS has done their job.
*   IRCC states they are waiting for "Partner Information" (CBSA).
*   CBSA refuses to provide updates, directing inquiries back to IRCC.
This circular shifting of responsibility creates a lack of accountability where CBSA’s Intelligence and Enforcement Branch effectively operates without a deadline or oversight.

**3. Withholding of Non-Sensitive Information:**
CBSA screenings primarily verify travel history and general criminality—activities far less sensitive than the national security assessments already completed by CSIS. Withholding the status of these administrative checks on "national security grounds" is an inappropriate application of security secrecy to cover administrative inaction.

### Request for Review
We respectfully request that NSICOP, under its mandate to review the **efficacy** of national security and intelligence activities, examine the following:

1.  **The operational efficiency of the CBSA’s [China and Americas] group** within the Centre for Immigration National Security Screening. specifically regarding the "holding" of files after CSIS assessments are complete.
2.  **The systemic delays** facing Chinese Study Permit applicants (specifically VIT 34 cases) and whether these delays constitute a policy of "subjective inaction" rather than legitimate security processing.
3.  **The lack of an oversight mechanism** for CBSA security screening delays, given that the PCRC is not active and NSIRA/PSIC have declined jurisdiction.

We urge the Committee to investigate why the CBSA is failing to meet its mandate in a timely manner, thereby jeopardizing the education of high-potential students and the reputation of Canada’s immigration system.

Respectfully,

[Your Name]
[Your Phone Number]
[Your Email Address]
*On behalf of 16 admitted students to the University of British Columbia*

**3. Withholding of Non-Sensitive Information:**  
CBSA screenings primarily verify travel history and general criminality—activities far less sensitive than the national security assessments already completed by CSIS. Withholding the status of these administrative checks on "national security grounds" is an inappropriate application of security secrecy to cover administrative inaction.
  
### **后面加一点：我们案子特别在于CSIS已经完成了对我们的安全审查。CSIS才是安全调查的主要负责部门，CBSA的安全调查权限和范围显然没有CSIS高和广，CSIS都能在平均两个月的时间内完成**
我们的安调，CBSA却拖延6个月乃至更久，实在匪夷所思，最轻的猜想指控是他们效率极其低下，更严重的指控只能是他们对中国国籍申请者主观上故意拖延不作为尸位素餐

这段补充非常有力。通过对比CSIS（主）和CBSA（辅）的效率，能够非常有逻辑地证明CBSA目前的拖延是不合理的。

在英文正式投诉中，我们将"尸位素餐"（dereliction of duty/gross negligence）和"主观故意"（deliberate inaction）用非常严厉但合乎行政逻辑的语言表达出来，迫使NSICOP去思考这背后的动机。

以下是修改后的 **第3点**，以及紧接着的结论段落：

***

**Revised Section 3 (修改后的第三部分):**

> **3. Disproportionate Delay Compared to CSIS Mandate:**
> It is critical to highlight that **CSIS**—Canada’s primary intelligence service with the highest security mandate and broadest investigative scope—has **already completed** its assessment of our files. On average, CSIS completed these comprehensive national security investigations within approximately two months.
>
> In stark contrast, the CBSA, whose role in this context is limited primarily to admissibility enforcement (such as travel history and criminality checks), has stalled our cases for over six months. It is professionally inexplicable that a secondary, administrative review would take three times longer than the primary intelligence investigation conducted by CSIS.
>
> This glaring disparity leads to serious concerns regarding the integrity of the process. At best, it indicates **extreme operational inefficiency** within the [China and Americas] group. At worst, it suggests a **subjective, systemic policy of deliberate inaction** targeting Chinese nationals, amounting to a dereliction of duty.

***

**Full Updated Letter (完整更新后的投诉信):**

**To:**
The Secretariat of the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP)
Ottawa, Ontario
Canada

**Date:** [Insert Date]

**Subject:** Report of Systemic Inaction and Lack of Accountability within the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) regarding Security Screening of International Students

**Dear Members of the Committee,**

I am writing to formally bring to your attention a matter of systemic inefficiency and lack of accountability within the **Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)**, specifically within the Intelligence and Enforcement Branch (IEB). **I submit this report on behalf of a collective of 16 admitted students to the University of British Columbia (myself and 15 others)** to demonstrate that this is not an isolated grievance, but a widespread failure in processing protocols that warrants the Committee's urgent review.

I am fully aware that the mandate of the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP) is to review the legislative, regulatory, policy, administrative, and financial framework for national security and intelligence, and that the Committee does not typically investigate individual complaints.

However, we are approaching NSICOP to report a **systemic administrative failure**. We allege that the CBSA is operating in a regulatory vacuum, engaging in subjective inaction regarding security screenings for Chinese students, with no effective oversight mechanism currently in place to hold them accountable.

### Exhaustion of Other Review Mechanisms
We turn to NSICOP as a measure of last resort because all other avenues for oversight have been exhausted or are unavailable:
1.  **Internal CBSA Complaints:** Have yielded no substantive response.
2.  **NSIRA (National Security and Intelligence Review Agency):** Has declined to review our complaints.
3.  **PSIC (Public Sector Integrity Commissioner):** Has declined jurisdiction (Case Ref: PSIC-D-2025-1112), stating they do not review allegations of wrongdoing against specific individuals, even when presented as a group issue.
4.  **PCRC (Public Complaints and Review Commission):** This body, intended to provide oversight for CBSA, is not yet operational.
*(We can provide written evidence of these refusals upon request.)*

As such, **NSICOP is currently the only body with the jurisdiction** to review what we allege to be systemic, subjective inaction by the CBSA.

### The Systemic Issue
The **16 individuals** represented in this complaint share the following characteristics:
*   We are all Chinese citizens admitted to the University of British Columbia.
*   Our applications are processed by the IRCC Beijing Office.
*   We fall under the **VIT 34** category of security screening.
*   **Crucially: CSIS has completed its security assessment mandates** and has transmitted its advice to the CBSA.

Our files are now stalled at the **Centre for Immigration National Security Screening**, specifically within the **[China and Americas] group** of the CBSA’s Security Screening and Refugee Directorate.

While CSIS has fulfilled its duties, the CBSA has failed to process these files for periods ranging from **six months to over ten months** *after* receiving the data from CSIS.

### Evidence of Maladministration and Lack of Accountability
We allege that the CBSA is engaged in systemic delays that violate its own operational guidelines and lack transparency:

**1. Violation of Service Standards:**
According to the *CBSA Immigration Control (IC) Manual Chapter 1 – Security Screening Process Manual*, our cases fall under *Chapter 10, Screening Guidelines for Temporary Resident Applications*. The stated processing standards are generally within 20 days. Our wait times now exceed **half a year** with zero movement. This suggests a breakdown in the administrative framework governing the IEB.

**2. The "Black Box" of Accountability:**
Through Access to Information and Privacy (ATIP) requests, we have confirmed that our status is marked as **“Received by CBSA”** under *ADMISSIBILITIES – SECURITY – SUB ACTIVITIES: 1*. This status has remained unchanged for months. CBSA refuses to provide updates, creating a lack of accountability where CBSA’s Intelligence and Enforcement Branch effectively operates without a deadline or oversight.

**3. Disproportionate Delay Compared to CSIS Mandate:**
It is critical to highlight that **CSIS**—Canada’s primary intelligence service with the highest security mandate and broadest investigative scope—has **already completed** its assessment of our files. On average, CSIS completed these comprehensive national security investigations within approximately two months.
In stark contrast, the CBSA, whose role in this context is limited primarily to admissibility enforcement (such as travel history and criminality checks), has stalled our cases for over six months. It is professionally inexplicable that a secondary, administrative review would take three times longer than the primary intelligence investigation conducted by CSIS.
This glaring disparity leads to serious concerns regarding the integrity of the process. At best, it indicates **extreme operational inefficiency** within the [China and Americas] group. At worst, it suggests a **subjective, systemic policy of deliberate inaction** targeting Chinese nationals, amounting to a dereliction of duty.

### Request for Review
We respectfully request that NSICOP, under its mandate to review the **efficacy** of national security and intelligence activities, examine the following:

1.  **The operational efficiency of the CBSA’s [China and Americas] group** within the Centre for Immigration National Security Screening. specifically regarding the "holding" of files after CSIS assessments are complete.
2.  **The systemic delays** facing Chinese Study Permit applicants (specifically VIT 34 cases) and whether these delays constitute a policy of "subjective inaction" rather than legitimate security processing.
3.  **The lack of an oversight mechanism** for CBSA security screening delays, given that the PCRC is not active and NSIRA/PSIC have declined jurisdiction.

We urge the Committee to investigate why the CBSA is failing to meet its mandate in a timely manner, thereby jeopardizing the education of high-potential students and the reputation of Canada’s immigration system.

Respectfully,

[Your Name]
[Your Phone Number]
[Your Email Address]
*On behalf of 16 admitted students to the University of British Columbia*
